title: BACKPROPAGATION
sub: Visual exam summary — every section maps to a real quiz question.
source: Source · Tilburg — Introduction to Deep Learning · Lecture 2 (Activation Functions & Backpropagation)

sections.title: THE ALGORITHM: FORWARD THEN BACKWARD
sections.exam: Backprop = a forward pass that predicts the output, then a backward pass that updates every weight by gradient descent on the loss — it does NOT adjust weights randomly.
sections.ascii:
FORWARD  ─────────────────────────────────────────►  predict
 x ─►[W1]─► z1 ─►f─► a1 ─►[W2]─► z2 ─►f─► y_hat ─► L(y_hat , y)
 ◄─────────────────────────────────────────────────  BACKWARD: assign blame

   for every weight:   W  ←  W − eta · (∂L/∂W)
   ∂L/∂W is obtained by the CHAIN RULE, computed layer by layer
   (loss compares prediction y_hat to target y; gradient says how to nudge W)

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Loss vs weight: from a random start, each step moves the weights OPPOSITE to the gradient toward the minimum. Backprop just supplies that gradient.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: W ← W − eta·ΔW and b ← b − eta·Δb. The whole job of backprop is to compute ΔW and Δb for every layer — the gradient of the loss.

sections.title: CHAIN RULE & THE DELTA RECURSION
sections.exam: Each layer's error is delta^l = (delta^(l+1) · W^(l+1)) ⊙ f'(z^l): error from the NEXT layer × NEXT weights × LOCAL activation slope; this layer-wise decomposition is exactly what the chain rule buys.
sections.ascii:
┌── error from NEXT layer ──┐   ┌ local slope ┐
 delta^l_j  =  delta^(l+1)_i · W^(l+1)_ij  ·  f'(z^l_j)
               │              │              │
               │              │              └ ∂a^l/∂z^l  (activation derivative)
               │              └ ∂z^(l+1)/∂a^l = W^(l+1)  (next layer's weights)
               └ ∂L/∂z^(l+1) = delta of the NEXT layer (already computed)

 full chain for a hidden unit:
   ∂L/∂z^(l-1) = [ ∂L/∂z^l · ∂z^l/∂a^(l-1) ] · ∂a^(l-1)/∂z^(l-1)
                 └── first two terms = how a^(l-1) feeds the loss ──┘

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: If L=f(a) and a=g(z) then ∂L/∂z = ∂L/∂a · ∂a/∂z. Every backprop term is just this rule stacked across layers.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: ∂L/∂z^(l-1) splits into three factors; the FIRST TWO together are the contribution of activation a^(l-1) to the overall loss — the exact quiz answer.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: delta^l_j = delta^(l+1)_i · W^(l+1)_ij · f'(z^l_j). The recursion that lets you reuse the next layer's error to get this layer's.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Left box = error propagated from layer l (i.e. delta^(l+1)·W^(l+1)); right box = derivative of the activation f'(z). Names every factor.

sections.title: READING BACKPROP CODE
sections.exam: Decode each line by the chain rule: delta_output = output − target (BCE+sigmoid shortcut), error = delta·Wᵀ pushes error BACKWARD through the weights, ×f'(z) applies the local slope, aᵀ·delta is the weight gradient.
sections.ascii:
delta_output = output - target            # ∂L/∂z_out  (BCE+sigmoid simplifies)
      │  .dot(W_ho.T)                        # push error BACKWARD thru weights
      ▼                                       #   = chain rule, opposite of forward
error_hidden  ── shape [1, hidden_size]
      │  * activation_derivative(z_hidden)   # × LOCAL slope ∂a/∂z at each neuron
      ▼
delta_hidden
      │
 W_ho -= a_hidden.T.dot(delta_output)*lr    # weight grad, shape (hidden, output)
 b    -= delta * lr                          # bias grad = delta

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: ∂L/∂W = ∂L/∂a · ∂a/∂z · ∂z/∂W. The middle term ∂a/∂z is the sigmoid_derivative(z) you see multiplied in the code.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: delta = (∂L/∂a)·(∂f/∂z): loss derivative times activation derivative. That is exactly delta_output = derivative_of_loss * sigmoid_derivative.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: ∂L/∂W = delta · a^(n-1): the weight gradient is delta times the incoming activation — the a_hidden.T.dot(delta_output) line.

sections.title: LOSS ↔ OUTPUT ACTIVATION
sections.exam: Match the output head to the task: binary → 1 sigmoid node + binary cross-entropy (chosen over MSE for better gradients, probability meaning, numerical stability); a CE you minimise plus a log-likelihood you maximise combine as L = L1 − L2; softmax is the f that makes coefficients sum to 1.
sections.ascii:
BCE  vs  MSE  for binary classification — why BCE wins:
   ✔ better GRADIENTS (faster convergence)   ✔ outputs read as PROBABILITIES
   ✔ NUMERICAL stability near 0 and 1
   (NOT because it forces outputs into [0,1] — that is the sigmoid's job)

combine two heads:  L1 = cross-entropy (MINIMISE) ,  L2 = log-likelihood (MAXIMISE)
        minimise L1 and maximise L2   =>   L = L1 - L2

softmax(y2) over N units  =>  coefficients a_i sum to 1  =>  A = Σ a_i·A_i  (weighted avg)

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: The loss L measures the distance between the network's output and the target. The choice of L (and the output activation) sets the very first gradient.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: At the output layer ∂L/∂a · ∂f/∂z: the first gradient DEPENDS on the chosen output activation and loss — which is why you pair them by task.

sections.title: RELU vs SIGMOID & VANISHING GRADIENTS
sections.exam: ReLU = max(0,x) is piecewise-linear and promotes sparsity; it beats sigmoid on vanishing gradients because its derivative is exactly 1 for positive inputs, whereas the sigmoid derivative tends to 0 once |z| is large (it saturates).
sections.ascii:
SIGMOID  σ(z)      z<<0 ──┐                ┌── z>>0   flat tails => σ'(z) ≈ 0
                         │                │           gradient VANISHES
                  0.5 ───┼────/───────────┤
                         └─────────────────────────►  z

ReLU  max(0,x)     derivative = 1 for x>0  (gradient flows, no shrink)
         ╱                            = 0 for x<0  (sparse activations)
    ────╱
        0          NOTE: ReLU is NOT ln(1+e^x) — that curve is SOFTPLUS

figures.label: WEB DIAGRAM
figures.desc: The sharp blue ReLU = max(0,x) vs the smooth softplus = ln(1+e^x). Quiz trap: the ln(1+e^x) formula is softplus, NOT ReLU.

figures.label: WEB DIAGRAM
figures.desc: The S-curve flattens at both ends; on those flat tails the slope (derivative) is near zero, so gradients vanish through deep sigmoid stacks.

sections.title: WEIGHT INITIALISATION: XAVIER vs HE
sections.exam: Pick init by activation: sigmoid/tanh → Xavier (variance ≈ 1/n_in); ReLU → He, i.e. draw weights from Normal(0, 2/n_in) with biases set to 0 — never initialise all weights to 0.
sections.ascii:
goal: keep activation variance stable layer-to-layer (avoid vanish / explode)

  SIGMOID / TANH  ──►  XAVIER (Glorot)   var = 1 / n_in
  ReLU            ──►  HE (Kaiming)      var = 2 / n_in ,  bias = 0

  W ~ Normal(mean = 0 , var)            biases = 0
  NEVER all-zeros  →  every neuron identical, gradients identical, no learning
  (n_in = number of incoming connections to the layer)

figures.label: WEB DIAGRAM
figures.desc: Both schemes sample weights from a zero-mean normal; only the variance differs — 1/n_in for Xavier, 2/n_in for He. Wider spread = larger variance.

sections.title: WHAT TRAINING STORES (OPTIMIZER MEMORY)
sections.exam: Per iteration you store params + their gradients + any optimizer state; params per layer = in·out + out (the +out is the bias), and the per-parameter multiplier is SGD 2, momentum/Nesterov 3, Adam 4.
sections.ascii:
stored per iteration = (number of params)  ×  (optimizer multiplier)
params per layer = in * out  +  out          ← the +out is the BIAS term

  5→4→3    SGD :  (5*4+4)+(4*3+3) = 24+15 = 39      →  2 * 39 = 78
  8→5→3→2  Adam:  (8*5+5)+(5*3+3)+(3*2+2) = 71      →  4 * 71 = 284

  traps: forgetting biases (in*out only) · forgetting the gradient copy ·
         using the wrong multiplier for the optimizer

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: W ← W − eta·ΔW with ΔW = (a^(l-1))ᵀ·delta. Each iteration holds the params, their gradients (ΔW, Δb), plus any optimizer buffers.
